description: ClawHQ backlog — aligned with evolved strategy (2026-03-28). See docs/STRATEGY.md for strategic context.

items.title: Decompose CLI index.ts — 4,320 lines into per-command modules
items.intent: A 4,320-line CLI file is unmaintainable, untestable, and blocks parallel development. Each command should live in its own module so changes to one command don't risk breaking others.
items.description: Architecture review found src/cli/index.ts is 4,320 lines containing all command definitions, option parsing, and handler logic in a single file. This creates: (1) merge conflicts when multiple features touch CLI simultaneously, (2) impossible to unit test individual commands in isolation, (3) no clear ownership boundary between modules. Decomposition strategy: extract each command (or command group) into src/cli/commands/<name>.ts, with index.ts reduced to a thin registration layer that imports and wires commands to Commander.js.
steps: Audit src/cli/index.ts — catalog all commands and their line ranges
Create src/cli/commands/ directory
Extract each command group into its own file (e.g., deploy.ts, doctor.ts, blueprint.ts, skill.ts, cloud.ts)
Each command file exports a function that takes the Commander program and registers its commands
Reduce index.ts to imports + command registration + top-level error handler
Run npm test to confirm no regression
Run npm run build to confirm compilation succeeds
acceptanceCriteria: src/cli/index.ts is under 500 lines — purely command registration and top-level error handling
Each command group lives in src/cli/commands/<name>.ts
All existing CLI commands work identically — no user-visible behavior change
npm test and npm run build pass with no regression
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Gate 0 prerequisite. Unblocks FEAT-110 (multi-instance), FEAT-106 (process.exit cleanup), and FEAT-111 (ClawWall). Every sprint that touches CLI without this decomposition carries compound regression risk. Sprint tonight — first in queue.

items.title: Multi-instance support — deploy multiple agents on the same host with isolated ports, networks, and data dirs
items.intent: A single host must support multiple independent agents without conflicts. Port collisions, shared networks, or overlapping data dirs are deployment failures that must be prevented by construction.
items.description: ClawHQ currently assumes a single agent per host. Deploying a second agent on the same server (different user, different blueprint) will collide on ports, Docker network names, data directories, and image tags. This blocks the primary use case of running agents for multiple people on one powerful machine. Required: (1) per-agent deployment directory (~/.clawhq/<agent-name>/), (2) per-agent Docker network with unique name, (3) per-agent port allocation with collision detection in preflight, (4) per-agent image tags (openclaw:<agent-name>), (5) per-agent container names.
steps: Add instanceName?: string to WizardAnswers (src/design/configure/types.ts), BuildOptions (src/build/docker/types.ts), and ClawHQConfig (src/config/types.ts) — optional field, defaults to 'default' for backward compatibility

Add helper functions to src/config/defaults.ts: agentNetworkName(instanceName?) returns 'clawhq_net' for default, 'clawhq_{name}_net' otherwise; agentImageTag(instanceName?) returns 'openclaw:custom' for default, 'openclaw:{name}' otherwise

Update src/design/configure/generate.ts: replace const AGENT_NETWORK = 'clawhq_net' with call to agentNetworkName(answers.instanceName); thread instanceName into buildClawHQConfig(); default deployDir to ~/.clawhq/{name}/ when name is not 'default'
Update src/build/docker/compose.ts: add networkName parameter (default 'clawhq_net') to generateCompose(); replace two hardcoded 'clawhq_net' references with the parameter

Update src/build/docker/build.ts: replace constant STAGE2_TAG = 'openclaw:custom' with agentImageTag(options.instanceName); STAGE1_TAG stays 'openclaw:local' (shared base image); thread networkName to generateCompose(); update serializeYaml to handle dynamic network name

Fix src/build/launcher/preflight.ts: delete hardcoded const GATEWAY_PORT = GATEWAY_DEFAULT_PORT (line 22); add gatewayPort parameter to runPreflight(); pass through to checkPorts() — falls back to GATEWAY_DEFAULT_PORT when not provided
Update src/build/launcher/deploy.ts: thread gatewayPort from DeployOptions to runPreflight()

Add tests: build with instanceName 'john' produces image tag 'openclaw:john' and network 'clawhq_john_net'; preflight with custom port checks that port; generateBundle with instanceName produces correct network in compose config; two different instance names produce non-colliding artifacts
acceptanceCriteria: Two agents can be deployed on the same host with different names, ports, and data directories
clawhq init with instanceName 'john' creates ~/.clawhq/john/ with isolated config
Docker networks and image tags are unique per agent name — network: clawhq_{name}_net, image: openclaw:{name}
Preflight checks the configured gateway port, not the hardcoded default 18789
Omitting instanceName or using 'default' produces identical artifacts to current behavior (backward compatible)
Stage 1 Docker image (openclaw:local) is shared across all instances — only stage 2 is per-instance
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Gate 0 blocker for John's deploy. Deep code analysis confirmed 5 hardcoding locations. Design decision: service name stays 'openclaw' — compose project name (from dir basename) provides container isolation. Stage 1 image shared across instances. CLI --name flag and clawhq list deferred to Gate 1. Depends on FEAT-108.

items.title: End-to-end smoke test — run full user journey against real Docker on local machine
items.intent: 946 unit tests pass but nothing proves the software works. An e2e smoke test must run the actual user journey (install → init → build → up → doctor → down → destroy) against real Docker containers and real filesystem, catching every integration gap the unit tests hide.
items.description: Every external system interaction in ClawHQ is mocked in tests. Docker builds, container lifecycle, filesystem scaffolding, health checks, and config generation are tested in isolation but never together. The documented user journey has never been executed end-to-end. This is the LAUNCH GATE — a security tool with a security bug is dead on arrival. No compromises.
steps: Create test/e2e/ directory for end-to-end tests
Create a minimal test blueprint YAML that requires no external API keys (local Ollama or mock LLM)
Create a non-interactive init path or pre-canned config fixture that bypasses the wizard
Write smoke-test.sh or smoke-test.test.ts that executes each CLI command in sequence
Each step: capture exit code, stdout, stderr; assert expected outcomes (files created, container running, etc.)
Add npm run test:e2e script to package.json (separate from unit tests — requires Docker)
Run the smoke test, document every failure, add each as a backlog item
Integrate into CI as an optional job (requires Docker-in-Docker or privileged runner)
acceptanceCriteria: npm run test:e2e runs the full user journey: install → init → build → up → doctor → down → destroy
Test uses real Docker (not mocked) and real filesystem (temp directory, cleaned up after)
Test uses a minimal blueprint that requires no external API keys
Each step reports pass/fail with captured output for debugging
Test is separate from unit tests (does not run on npm test) because it requires Docker
Every failure found during initial run is filed as a backlog item
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: THE LAUNCH GATE. No public release without this. FAILED TWICE (failCount:2). Trust paradox — security tool with security bug is a brand-killer. Depends on FEAT-110 for correct directory structure (~/.clawhq/<agent-name>/). Each failure discovered new integration gaps — these are the bugs FEAT-018 is supposed to catch. AC verify commands added. Run smoke test completely before declaring pass.

items.title: ClawWall content sanitization tool — prompt injection defense for all inbound external content
items.intent: Every agent that reads external content (email, web, social media, RSS) must have a sanitization layer that filters prompt injection attacks before content reaches the LLM. This is not optional — it is a security-by-default requirement.
items.description: Clawdius has a battle-tested 'sanitize' tool (ClawWall) that filters all inbound content from X, Tavily, Substack, email, and news through a prompt injection firewall implementing OWASP LLM01 Tier 1+2 defenses. It logs, quarantines, and strips malicious content. ClawHQ has no equivalent — generated tools pass raw external content directly to the LLM context.
steps: Design the sanitize tool API: stdin filter, configurable strictness levels, quarantine log format
Implement sanitize as a generated tool (src/design/tools/sanitize.ts) included in every blueprint by default
Wire all external-facing tool generators (tavily, email, quote) to pipe through sanitize
Add quarantine log: ~/.clawhq/<agent>/security/quarantine.jsonl for blocked content
Add OWASP LLM01 Tier 1 patterns: system prompt overrides, role confusion, instruction injection
Add Tier 2 patterns: encoded payloads (base64, hex), Unicode homoglyphs, invisible characters
Add tests with real-world prompt injection examples
Add doctor check: verify sanitize is on PATH and quarantine log is writable
acceptanceCriteria: A sanitize tool is generated and placed on PATH for every new agent deployment
All external-facing tools (email, tavily, web search) pipe content through sanitize
Known prompt injection patterns (OWASP LLM01 Tier 1+2) are detected and stripped
Quarantine log records all filtered content with timestamps and source attribution
clawhq doctor checks sanitize availability and log writability
Sanitize tool works as a standalone stdin filter (composable with any tool)
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Security-by-default requirement. Trust paradox — an agent platform without content sanitization contradicts the security promise. Extracted from Clawdius production deployment. Depends on FEAT-108 for clean CLI integration. PO REVIEW 2026-03-28: Priority confirmed MUST — every blueprint reads external content; unfiltered prompt injection is not a theoretical risk. The quarantine log AC is critical: silent filtering erodes trust, operators need to see what was blocked. AC verify commands added.

items.title: SHA256 binary pinning in Docker build — verify every downloaded tool binary against known hashes
items.intent: Every binary downloaded during Docker build must be verified against a pinned SHA256 hash. Unverified downloads in a security-by-default platform are a supply chain vulnerability.
items.description: ClawHQ's Docker build stage downloads tool binaries (himalaya, gh, curl, jq, ripgrep, etc.) via HTTPS URLs but does not verify checksums. A compromised CDN or GitHub release could inject malicious binaries into every ClawHQ-built agent.
steps: Add a binary manifest to the build config: tool name, version, URL, SHA256 hash
Update Dockerfile generator (src/build/docker/build.ts) to emit SHA256 verification after each download
Add hash verification: download → sha256sum → compare → fail build if mismatch
Populate manifest with current hashes for all tool binaries (himalaya, gh, curl, jq, rg, git, ffmpeg)
Add clawhq build --verify-hashes flag to re-check hashes against upstream (for maintainer use)
Add tests: build fails on hash mismatch, build succeeds on correct hash
acceptanceCriteria: Every binary downloaded in Docker build is verified against a pinned SHA256 hash
Build fails immediately if any hash does not match
Binary manifest is human-readable and auditable
clawhq build output shows verification status for each binary
Hash mismatch produces a clear error naming the tool, expected hash, and actual hash
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Security-by-default. Supply chain vulnerability contradicts core promise. Extracted from Clawdius Dockerfile which pins every binary with SHA256. PO REVIEW 2026-03-28: Priority confirmed MUST — an unverified binary in a security-by-default platform is a brand-killing contradiction. AC verify commands added.

items.title: Sentinel monitoring service — upstream intelligence that a cron job can't replicate
items.intent: ClawHQ must have a day-one revenue hypothesis. Sentinel is a paid monitoring service that provides upstream intelligence — pre-computing config breakage against incoming OpenClaw commits, tracking CVE impact per blueprint, and alerting on skill dependency changes. This must do something a local cron job cannot.
items.description: The evolved strategy requires testing revenue from day one, not deferring it. Sentinel is the hypothesis: ~$19/month monitoring that enhances sovereignty rather than compromising it. CRITICAL DESIGN CONSTRAINT: this must NOT be 'clawhq doctor --watch' as a service — that's replicable with a cron job and the sovereignty audience will call it out immediately. The value is upstream intelligence: (1) monitor OpenClaw GitHub commits and pre-compute whether your specific config will break before you update, (2) track new CVEs and map them to your installed skills/tools, (3) detect when upstream dependencies of your skills change, (4) aggregate anonymized health signals across the fleet to identify emerging issues before they hit you. Phase 1 (MVP): OpenClaw commit monitoring + config breakage prediction. Phase 2: CVE mapping + skill dependency tracking.
steps: Design Sentinel architecture: upstream monitor (GitHub API polling), config analyzer (diff against user's blueprint), alert delivery (email/webhook)
Implement OpenClaw commit monitor: poll GitHub API for new commits to main, parse changed files, identify config-impacting changes
Implement config breakage predictor: given a commit diff and a user's compiled config, determine if the update would break their deployment
Implement alert system: email or webhook notification with specific breakage details and recommended action
Design pricing page / signup flow for revenue validation
Build minimal web endpoint for Sentinel subscription management
Add clawhq cloud sentinel connect command to link a deployment to Sentinel
Phase 2: CVE tracker that maps advisories to user's specific skill/tool inventory
acceptanceCriteria: Sentinel monitors OpenClaw upstream commits and identifies config-impacting changes
Users receive alerts BEFORE updating when a new release would break their specific config
Alert includes: what changed upstream, what would break in their config, recommended action
Service provides value that a local cron job cannot replicate (upstream intelligence, not local health checks)
Pricing page exists and accepts signups for revenue validation
Revenue signal measurable within 30 days of announcement
items.groomingNotes: PO REVIEW 2026-03-29. ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS (PO-owned): (1) WHERE IT RUNS: Sentinel is a clawhq.com backend service, not a self-hosted daemon. Users connect to it via 'clawhq cloud sentinel connect'. (2) ALERT DELIVERY: user provides email or webhook URL at signup; stored in clawhq.com user account (not local). (3) GITHUB API AUTH: Sentinel uses a single authenticated GitHub App or PAT at the backend — users do not need to provide GitHub credentials. (4) PRICING: 9/month, validated against assumptions.json targets. OPEN FOR TECH LEAD: config breakage prediction algorithm (diff approach vs. semantic analysis). Still NOT READY — Tech Lead must design the breakage prediction mechanism and answer: how does a commit diff get evaluated against a user's compiled config? Options: (a) schema diff against user's blueprint YAML, (b) file-path pattern matching against commit changed files, (c) semantic analysis of commit messages. PO preference: start with (b) as Phase 1 — it is simple and catches 80% of breaking changes. Re-groom when Tech Lead has a design.

items.title: OpenClaw Security Incident Tracker — public authority-building resource
items.intent: ClawHQ must become the authoritative source on OpenClaw security incidents before launch. This builds the exact audience that will adopt ClawHQ and creates compounding discovery value that one-time launch events cannot.
items.description: The OpenClaw security landscape is alarming: 9+ CVEs in first 2 months, 20-36% of ClawHub skills found malicious, 42,000+ exposed instances, 14 silent config landmines. Nobody is tracking this systematically. A public incident tracker builds authority, drives SEO, and creates the audience that ClawHQ serves. This should run IN PARALLEL with development, not as a contingency. Content: CVE tracker with impact analysis, malicious skill registry, exposed instance statistics (anonymized), breaking change log with migration guides. Can start as a curated markdown document and evolve into a live dashboard.
steps: Create docs/security/ directory for public security content
Compile initial CVE list: all known OpenClaw CVEs with severity, impact, and ClawHQ mitigation status
Compile malicious skill analysis: known malicious ClawHub items, detection patterns, ClawHQ's vetting response
Compile exposed instance data: methodology, current count, common misconfigurations found
Create docs/security/INCIDENTS.md as the public-facing tracker
Add RSS/JSON feed generation for automated consumption
Set up monthly update cadence (ties into development-as-content)
Cross-reference each incident with ClawHQ's specific mitigation (doctor check, config validation, etc.)
acceptanceCriteria: Public incident tracker exists with all known OpenClaw CVEs and their ClawHQ mitigation status
Each incident includes: description, severity, affected versions, ClawHQ mitigation, detection method
Tracker is linkable and SEO-friendly (individual incident anchors)
Content demonstrates deep OpenClaw security expertise
Monthly update cadence established
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Authority-building play that addresses discovery wound. Better than a fallback — should run in parallel with development. The OpenClaw security landscape data already exists in docs/OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md and docs/PROBLEMS.md. This is about packaging it as a public resource. Sprint-ready — primarily content work, not code.

items.title: Development-as-content pipeline — every commit generates discoverable content
items.intent: ClawHQ's development work must produce discoverable content as a byproduct. One-time launch events decay in 72 hours. Compounding content solves the discovery problem without a separate content calendar.
items.description: The evolved strategy identified discovery decay as a critical wound. The fix: every development action produces content. Bug fix → postmortem article. New blueprint → tutorial walkthrough. OpenClaw breaking change → 'what broke and how we fixed it.' This is a process change, not a feature. Implementation: (1) CHANGELOG entries written as narratives, not version lists, (2) template for converting commits/PRs into technical articles, (3) docs/articles/ directory for long-form content derived from development, (4) PROBLEMS.md already exists and is evergreen SEO — formalize the update process.
steps: Create docs/articles/ directory for technical content
Create article template: title, problem, context, solution, what we learned, ClawHQ relevance
Rewrite CHANGELOG.md entries as narratives (not just version bumps)
Write first article from existing work: '14 Ways Your OpenClaw Agent Silently Breaks' (from PROBLEMS.md content)
Write second article from BUG-125: 'Why Regex YAML Patching Will Corrupt Your Docker Config'
Add CONTRIBUTING.md section on development-as-content expectations
Document the process: each PR that fixes a landmine or adds a blueprint should include a content draft
acceptanceCriteria: docs/articles/ directory exists with article template
At least 2 articles written from existing development work
CHANGELOG.md entries are narrative, not version lists
Process documented in CONTRIBUTING.md for future development
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Zero-cost discovery fix. The content already exists in the codebase — PROBLEMS.md, OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md, bug fixes. This is about packaging it. Sprint-ready — content work, not code. The '14 ways' article could be the HN launch piece.

items.title: Assumption validation framework — concrete tests for the 3 riskiest strategic assumptions
items.intent: Strategy without validation is fiction. The 3 highest-risk assumptions must have concrete pass/fail criteria, measurement methods, and deadlines so the team knows when to pivot.
items.description: The evolved strategy identified 7 assumptions, 3 critical. Each needs a concrete test: (1) Addressable demand — measure clone→install conversion in first 1,000 visitors (pass: >5%), (2) Willingness to pay — Sentinel landing page signups (pass: 10+ in 60 days), (3) Foundation absorption — monitor OpenClaw PRs for guided config (signal: 2+ PRs in 6 months). Implementation: tracking dashboard or simple JSON file that records metrics against targets, reviewed monthly.
steps: Create backlog/assumptions.json with the 3 testable assumptions, pass/fail criteria, and deadlines
Add GitHub repo analytics tracking plan (stars, clones, installs via curl script)
Design Sentinel landing page for willingness-to-pay test
Set up OpenClaw GitHub watch for configuration-related PRs/RFCs
Document review cadence: monthly assumption review, update assumptions.json with latest data
acceptanceCriteria: backlog/assumptions.json documents all 3 assumptions with pass/fail criteria and deadlines
Each assumption has a concrete measurement method that doesn't require guessing
Monthly review cadence is documented
First review date set for 30 days after repo goes public
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Converts 'direction locked' into something testable. Without this, the strategy is faith, not science. Sprint-ready — primarily documentation work. Can be done in a single sprint.

items.title: Reduce process.exit() usage in CLI — return exit codes or throw for testability
items.intent: Make CLI command handlers testable and allow proper cleanup. Commands should throw or return exit codes instead of calling process.exit() directly.
items.description: Architecture review found 192 direct process.exit() calls. This prevents testing error paths, blocks defer/cleanup handlers, and makes graceful shutdown impossible.
steps: Create a CommandError class that carries an exit code
Replace process.exit() calls with throw CommandError in command handlers
Add try/finally blocks around spinner usage to ensure cleanup
Wire top-level parseAsync catch to extract exit codes from CommandError
Add unit tests for error paths in key commands (deploy, destroy, scan)
acceptanceCriteria: Command handlers throw errors or return exit codes instead of calling process.exit()
A single top-level error handler in the CLI entry point calls process.exit()
Spinners are properly stopped in error paths via try/finally
At least 3 command handlers have unit tests covering error paths
items.groomingNotes: Blocked by FEAT-108. After decomposition, each command file can be fixed independently. process.exit() cleanup is per-command surgery — doing it in a 4320-line index.ts carries high regression risk.

items.title: 1Password integration — agent-accessible credential vault via service account tokens
items.intent: Agents need credential access for email, logins, API calls. Hardcoded env vars have no rotation or audit. 1Password service accounts give scoped read-only access with full audit trail — aligned with data sovereignty and security-by-default.
items.description: Integrate 1Password service account model into agent runtime. Architecture: user's 1Password account has an Agent Vault with only the credentials the agent needs. Agent gets a service account token (OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN) injected as Docker secret. op CLI or Python SDK installed in container. Helper utility claw-secret wraps credential fetch with logging. Security model: read-only, fetch-at-use (never persisted), never in LLM context, scoped vault, full audit, instant revoke, quarterly rotation.
steps: Add op CLI or 1Password Python SDK to the Docker build stage (src/build/docker/)
Add OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN to credential store (src/secure/credentials/) with Docker secret injection
Create claw-secret helper utility that wraps op read with logging and never exposes credentials to stdout/LLM context
Add credential probe for 1Password service account token validity (src/secure/credentials/probes.ts)
Add blueprint configuration for 1Password vault name and credential mapping
Add doctor check: 1Password service account token valid, vault accessible, op CLI installed
Add tests for credential fetch, token validation, error handling
acceptanceCriteria: op CLI or Python SDK available in agent container after clawhq build
Service account token injected via Docker secrets, never in env vars or config files
claw-secret utility fetches credentials with audit logging
Credentials never appear in LLM context, agent memory files, or logs
clawhq doctor checks 1Password connectivity and token validity
Blueprint supports 1Password vault configuration
items.groomingNotes: From GitHub issue #8. Core credential access pattern needed for any agent that interacts with external services. Aligns with security-by-default and data sovereignty.

items.title: Construct meta-skill — autonomous self-improvement loop for agent capability growth
items.intent: Agents must grow their capabilities over time without manual intervention. The construct skill is the mechanism: assess gaps → propose new skills → build → deploy → validate. This is THE differentiator for the 'agent grows' pillar.
items.description: Clawdius has a 'construct' skill that runs daily at 2am PT. Over 3 months it has autonomously built 7 skills. ClawHQ's skill system supports install/remove/update but has no autonomous growth mechanism.
steps: Design construct skill config for ClawHQ: SKILL.md, state machine (assess/propose/build/deploy/validate), cron schedule
Add construct to configs/skills/ as a built-in skill
Implement state persistence (construct tracks what it has assessed, proposed, built)
Wire construct into blueprint cron_config as an optional daily job
Add skill vetting integration — construct-built skills must pass the same vetting as manually installed skills
Add construct to 'replace-my-pa', 'founders-ops', and 'research-copilot' blueprints by default
Add tests for state machine transitions and skill output validation
acceptanceCriteria: Construct skill is available as a built-in skill in configs/skills/
Construct follows Assess→Propose→Build→Deploy→Validate cycle
Construct-built skills pass the same vetting pipeline as manually installed skills
State is persisted across runs (no redundant assessments)
Blueprints can opt-in to construct via skill_bundle configuration
At least 3 blueprints include construct by default
items.groomingNotes: TECH LEAD GROOM 2026-03-29. NOT READY. Blocking design question: how does construct-built skill vetting integrate with the existing skill vetting pipeline in src/evolve/skills/? The vetting pipeline must be confirmed to exist and be hookable before this can sprint. Also: state persistence format (sqlite vs. JSON file) needs decision — affects the assess/propose/build/deploy/validate machine design. Recommend: architect creates a design doc or ADR for the vetting pipeline integration, then re-groom. Current steps are correct at a high level but too vague for a developer to follow without guessing.

items.title: Operational automation scripts — auto-update, security monitoring, workspace backup generation
items.intent: A deployed agent must maintain itself. Auto-updates, security advisory monitoring, and workspace backups should be generated by ClawHQ, not hand-written by the operator.
items.description: Clawdius has 24 operational scripts. ClawHQ has doctor and backup commands but does not generate the operational automation layer.
steps: Generate auto-update script during clawhq init
Generate security monitor script (checks GitHub Security Advisories for OpenClaw CVEs)
Generate workspace backup script (incremental rsync to configurable backup dir)
Generate systemd unit files for firewall watcher and backup timer
Add clawhq ops install command to deploy generated scripts and timers
Add doctor checks: auto-update timer active, backup recent, security monitor running
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq init generates operational scripts in the deployment directory
clawhq ops install deploys scripts as systemd timers/services
Auto-update runs on configurable schedule (default: daily)
Security monitor checks GitHub Advisories and alerts on critical/high CVEs
Workspace backup runs on configurable schedule with retention policy
clawhq doctor reports operational automation status
items.groomingNotes: TECH LEAD GROOM 2026-03-29. NOT READY. Multi-instance design needed: if two agents are on the same host, do they share systemd timers or have per-instance timers? Per-instance timers (clawhq-autoupdate-{instanceName}.timer) seems correct but needs explicit decision. Also: clawhq ops install command doesn't exist yet — it's a new CLI entry point. Need to confirm if src/cli/ops.ts exists or if it needs to be created. Check src/cli/ structure before marking ready. Script generation steps are clear; the install command and timer naming are the blocking questions.

items.title: Domain-based egress firewall with ipset — upgrade from IP-based iptables to DNS-resolved allowlists
items.intent: Agent egress must be restricted to explicitly allowed domains, not IP addresses. IP-based rules miss CDN rotations, multi-homed services, and IPv6.
items.description: ClawHQ's firewall module uses IP-based iptables rules. Clawdius uses domain-based egress: a domain allowlist resolved via DNS into an ipset, refreshed every 5 minutes.
steps: Add domain-allowlist concept to blueprint security_posture
Generate domain allowlist file from blueprint egress config during clawhq init
Implement ipset creation from domain allowlist
Update firewall module to use ipset match rules instead of per-IP iptables rules
Generate refresh mechanism for periodic DNS re-resolution
Add doctor check: ipset exists, not stale, matches allowlist
Add tests for domain resolution, ipset creation, stale detection
acceptanceCriteria: Blueprint security posture includes a domain allowlist
clawhq up creates an ipset from DNS resolution of the allowlist
iptables rules reference the ipset, not individual IP addresses
A refresh mechanism keeps the ipset current
clawhq doctor detects stale ipsets
clawhq down removes the ipset and iptables rules cleanly
items.groomingNotes: TECH LEAD GROOM 2026-03-29. Multi-instance design resolved: each instance gets its own ipset named clawhq_{instanceName}_egress. instanceName comes from blueprint/config name (already available in FirewallOptions). Atomic refresh via ipset swap eliminates traffic gaps during DNS re-resolution. Current firewall.ts already has applyFirewall() and removeFirewall() — changes are additive. ipset CLI must be installed (add to installer pre-reqs check). AC verify commands target sudo — tests require privileged mode or mocked execFileAsync. Sprint-ready.

items.title: Integration tests for credential probes — test against real API endpoints with test keys
items.intent: Credential health probes are tested with mocked fetch. If an API changes its auth error response, our probe will misinterpret it. Real probes against real APIs must be tested.
items.description: Live probe tests that run when test API keys are available. Ollama probe can always run locally.
steps: Add integration test: src/secure/credentials/probes.integration.test.ts
For each probe: if env var with test key is set, run real probe
Ollama probe can always run if Ollama is installed locally
Validate: probe returns valid:true for good keys, valid:false for bad keys
Skip gracefully when no test keys are available
acceptanceCriteria: Live probe tests run against real APIs when test credentials are available
Ollama probe runs against local Ollama when available
Tests skip gracefully when no credentials are provided
Existing mock-based unit tests remain unchanged
items.groomingNotes: TECH LEAD GROOM 2026-03-29. AC verify commands added. Skip-if-no-key pattern: probes.ts already returns {ok:false, message:'Not configured'} when env var absent — test harness checks this and calls test.skip(). Ollama probe: check localhost:11434/api/tags availability before running. File: src/secure/credentials/probes.integration.test.ts. Import directly from probes.ts source — no need to build first if ts-jest is configured (it is). Sprint-ready.

items.title: Stoic coach personality blueprint — purpose-built agent for philosophical mentorship
items.intent: The stoic coach blueprint proves ClawHQ can forge agents with deep personality — not just task automation, but genuine character. This is the blueprint that makes people say 'this is different from ChatGPT'.
items.description: Extract Clawdius's stoic identity patterns into a 'Stoic Coach' blueprint. Part of the '10 curated blueprints' strategy — this is blueprint #8.
steps: Create configs/blueprints/stoic-coach.yaml
Define personality: high analyticalDepth, moderate warmth, high directness, low verbosity
Define customization questions: philosophical tradition, practice times, accountability style
Define toolbelt: journal (append-only markdown), reflection-prompt generator
Define skills: daily-stoic (morning prompt + evening reflection), weekly-review
Define cron, autonomy, and memory policy
Test: clawhq init --guided with stoic-coach produces a deployable agent config
acceptanceCriteria: stoic-coach.yaml is a complete blueprint with all required sections
Personality dimensions are distinct from other blueprints
Blueprint includes at least one unique tool and one unique skill
clawhq init --guided with stoic-coach produces a deployable agent config
The forged agent has a distinctive voice — tested by reading generated SOUL.md
items.groomingNotes: TECH LEAD GROOM 2026-03-29. stoic-coach.yaml does not exist. Journal tool does not exist in src/design/tools/ — must be created as part of this sprint. daily-stoic and weekly-review skills must be created. Blueprint format is fully established from 10 existing blueprints — use email-manager.yaml as structural template. items.title: JMAP email backend — FastMail-compatible email tool as alternative to himalaya/IMAP
items.intent: Users on FastMail (or any JMAP provider) must have a native email tool that uses JMAP instead of IMAP.
items.description: Clawdius has a battle-tested email-fastmail.py (stdlib Python, no deps) implementing JMAP. ClawHQ's email tool generator only produces himalaya-based IMAP scripts.
steps: Add JMAP email backend to src/design/tools/email.ts
Generate email-jmap.py (stdlib Python, no deps)
Add JMAP credential collection to wizard
Add account switching support (--account flag)
Add doctor check: JMAP session endpoint reachable, token valid
Add to email-manager and replace-my-pa blueprints
acceptanceCriteria: Email tool generator can produce JMAP-based scripts as alternative to himalaya
Generated JMAP tool uses stdlib Python only
Account switching works between JMAP and himalaya backends
clawhq doctor checks JMAP connectivity when configured
items.groomingNotes: TECH LEAD GROOM 2026-03-29. Coexistence design: email (himalaya) and email-jmap are separate tool entries in TOOL_GENERATORS — no shared state, no backend switching logic needed. Blueprint authors choose which tool to include. Credential types are separate (himalaya uses HIMALAYA_BIN env var; JMAP uses jmapBaseUrl+accountId+token in credentials.json). JMAP discovery: .well-known/jmap returns session object with primaryAccounts map — use accountId from session or from user config. FastMail primary endpoint: api.fastmail.com. No X tool or news tool dependency. Sprint-ready.

items.title: News aggregator tool — multi-source scan with deduplication and interest-graph filtering
items.intent: Agents that monitor information sources need a unified news aggregator. Foundation for any monitoring or research blueprint.
items.description: Clawdius has a 'news' tool that scans X + Substack + Tavily by interest topic, deduplicates, and manages API budget.
steps: Design news tool API: scan by interest, deduplicate, budget management
Design interests tool API: add/remove topics, set priorities, manage sources
Add tool generators to src/design/tools/
Generate interests.json with default interests from blueprint
Add to research-copilot, founders-ops blueprints
Add tests for deduplication logic and budget management
acceptanceCriteria: News tool scans and returns deduplicated, filtered results
Interests tool manages a persistent interest graph
Generated tools use existing tool dependencies as backends
Budget management prevents runaway API costs
items.groomingNotes: TECH LEAD GROOM 2026-03-29. NOT READY. Partial dependencies met: tavily tool exists in src/design/tools/tavily.ts. X (Twitter) tool does NOT exist — no src/design/tools/x.ts or twitter.ts found. Substack has no tool either. Deduplication logic needs a key design decision (hash by URL? by title+source?). Budget management needs a design (per-run token budget or daily cap?). Recommend: groom a prerequisite item to add the X/Substack source adapter, or scope news tool to tavily-only for the first sprint. As written, the item depends on tools that don't exist.
